All right, so we're gonna start tasting up here. I'm just about ready to go. What are these Epicurean experts tasting so earnestly? Vintage white wines, fine Russian vodka's? Nope, they're putting their professional taste buds to work analyzing the finer points of bottled water. See them crazy? Last year, Americans alone spent over $4 billion on bottled water. Why not drink it from the faucet at pennies on the dollar? I like it because it's definitely got less pollutants, looking to not have a lot of heavy metals in my water and generally I can find that in bottled water. I think it's cleaner than our regular tap water. You know, it's not as impure as some of the tap water can be. Some of the tap water I really don't trust. People are convinced bottled water is safer than tap water. That's exactly what the bottled water companies want us to believe. They all be bullshit. We sure do love these bottles. Let me just get a footing. Okay, here we go. There we go. Bottle water is regulated by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration as a packaged food product. Stephen Kay, Spokesman International Bottle Water Association. The FDA sets very specific and stringent standards for safety, quality, and labeling that help ensure the safety and quality of the bottled water products that consumers enjoy. Many people in the public are persuaded incorrectly that it's a lot safer and it's a lot cleaner than tap water. I'm Eric Olson. I'm a drinking water expert with the Natural Resources Defense Council. Just because you're going into the grocery store, pulling a bottle off the shelf and spending a buck for the bottle or two bucks for the bottle, doesn't mean that that water is really any better for you than what you're turning on your tap and getting out of your tap. The NRDC conducted a comprehensive four-year scientific study of 1,000 brands of bottled water. Incredibly, the results showed that one-third of the bottled waters violated their own industry standards for water quality. It's clear that just because the water is coming out of the bottle doesn't mean it doesn't have bacteria in it. A lot of it did when we tested it, and it doesn't mean it doesn't have arsenic in it. A lot of the bottled water did have arsenic in it. Bacteria? Arsenic? We're betting it's harmless traits amounts, but a thousand times the price of tap water for that shit? Shouldn't the standards be higher? By law, bottle water standards must be at least as stringent and protective of public health as environmental protection agency standards for public water. If the EPA doesn't enforce bottled water regulations, the FDA does, and there's a big difference. The Environmental Protection Agency has hundreds of people to regulate tap water. FDA has less than one person. There really is no cop on the beat for bottled water. There really is no one keeping an eye on the industry. Did you hear that? Less than one person. I guess that means he's spending some of his time checking out the bags of sand and they're going to try to sell us next. Another thing, the bottled water companies enjoy a convenient loophole in the system. FDA says they don't regulate any bottled water that's bottled and sold within the same state. So many states really have no bottled water regulatory program at all. Okay, purity and safety aren't good reasons to drink bottled water, but there must be some reason. Tap water is not always the best tasting. I don't like the taste of front-end water. Bottled water tastes better at least, and it looks better, so I'm shaking. Of course, the taste. Bottle water tastes better than tap water, right? We went to New York City to conduct one of our famous unscientific bullshit tests. No expense was spared as we filled bottle A with water from an ordinary New York faucet while bottle B contained a relatively expensive store-bought bottled water. First, our taste testers tried the bottled water. It's a little bitter. A little bitter, yes. This is too sweet. Is it a little America? Yes, bitter. Then they drank the water from the faucet. They go like this, better. It's better. This one is better. This one is the A. A. A is New York tap water. No way! Way! 75% of the people who took our test picked New York's tap water over bottled water. We've now ruled out safety, purity, and taste. Why would people pay such a premium for bottled water? Is it all in their minds? To find out we took over a very trendy California restaurant. We hid our cameras on the balcony to watch. Instead of a wine steward, we created the world's first water steward. We printed our own elegant water menus with phony imported waters, costing as much as $7 per bottle. What was the actual source of these chic waters? A garden hose on the restaurant patio. I love this fucking show. Good evening, folks. Hi, how are you doing? Welcome. My name is Tim, and I will be your water steward tonight. Cool. The water steward gives our first lucky couple our special water list. Like a wine list. What do you want to try? You want to try this? Sounds French? Yeah. Well, I guess we'll get the L'Adu Rebonne. The L'Adu Rebonne? Yeah. Oh, fantastic. It's French for tap water. And for you, sir, would you like to try one, maybe compare notes? Yeah, do the other one. Sure, I'll do the Mount Fuji. The Mount Fuji? Ah, two excellent choices. Thank you, guys. Okay. Yeah, I'm just kidding. It has a flavor to it. How would you compare it to tap water? Oh, yeah, definitely better than tap water. It doesn't have an aftertaste. It's got a flavor that it almost feels like a beverage other than water without sugar or any additives. L'Adu Rebonne passed the taste test. How about Mount Fuji? Which of course came from the same funky oz. It's right from the top of Mount Fuji. It's a very pure water. It's a natural diuretic and an antitoxin. It does feel glacier-like. It does. Glacier-oriented. Glacier-like. Yeah. This is good. Yeah. I've taken the liberty of bringing you a bottle of our Agua de Culo. Okay. It's 650 per bottle. Mmm. I think it's got a very fresh taste to it. It's crisper. It has a crisp. It's like a cake. Surely this couple wasn't representative. Let's see how some other people rated our premium hose water. It's really not good. It's just smoother than tap water. It's better than that Lake County water. We're pretty sure the hose added that glacial taste that was so widely praised. Consumers are offered water with names conveying pure and pristine water sources. Ever hear of Alaskan Falls water? That must come from the crystal clear glacier waters of our 49th state, right? Sorry, folks. Alaskan flows from this Liquibox Corporation building in Worthington, Ohio. Does the brand Yosemite conjure up visions of the cool, pristine waters rushing through California's high Sierras? Well then, the marketers have done their jobs. The source of Yosemite bottled water is actually 400 miles away in northeast Los Angeles. How about Everest water? Could our piddly show possibly afford to send a camera crew to Nepal? Not necessary. Everest comes from the industrial section of Corpus Christi, Texas. In fact, if you read the fine print on the fucking label, they even admit the water comes from a municipal source. That is tap water, brothers and sisters of the cult of the bottle. Hey, should we get a discount on bottled water directly from municipal water sources? We paid for the filters and treatments these guys are selling back to us. Safe tap water isn't free. We in the U.S. already paid for that great, clean tap water through fees and taxes. Let me get a little help on here. In much of the rest of the world, tap water equals rapid, severe weight loss with the introduction of new, possibly harmful friends to your intestines or organs. Are we so ashamed of our wonderful tap water? We need to disguise it. I love these bottles. Oh, that would have been good, but I slipped. The big bucks in bottling tap water have even convinced soft drink giants to take the plunge. Coke calls theirs the Sade. Pepsi makes aquafina. Pepsi was the first to see it and got involved. They use their existing marketing and their existing transportation and so on. And they're making money hand over fist just by basically selling Coke or Pepsi without the syrup in it for as much or more than they sell the Coke or Pepsi. Think about it. What other product can you take out of the ground for almost nothing, put in a fancy bottle, and then sell to people for more than gasoline? It's almost like Britain Money. Tell them now we're trying to figure out how to get in on the action. Then we remembered our hose. Back in the restaurant, we had one more very special bottle of water for our diners to sample. One with a unique ingredient. Amazon is filtered by the Brazilian Rainforest Natural Filtration System. This particular water, if you'll notice, has one of the oldest creatures of the Amazon rainforest placed in the bottom of the bottle. The Brazilian spotted arachnid. It's been purified. A spider, yes. It's called the Amazonian arachnid. It's purified. It's absolutely no toxins involved in the water. It actually adds to the medicinal qualities of the water itself. Why would anyone outside of the Mojave even consider drinking water with a spider in it? Because, just like with real bottle water companies, it's all in how you sell it. So how did our diners compare the various bottles of hose water they sampled? What do you think about the Amazon? I just thought it was fresher. It's about pure... Okay, now I can taste the difference on this one. I can taste the difference too. This is definitely you can taste the minerals. It's a harder water, I guess, you know? And this one, I don't taste the minerals so much. Well, maybe it was time to let the diners know just what they've been drinking. Now, what would you think if I told you that all of these waters came from the same garden hose? I actually filled the bottles myself. No, you didn't. I did. You're kidding me. Again? All these waters came from the water hose. I'm sorry, Phil. So if people want to waste all their money on personal bottles of water, so what? No harm, right? Well, I guess we could worry about the gazillion of pesky empty water bottles sitting in refuse dumps worldwide, but that landfill crisis, it's a little bit of bullshit too. You can make cool stuff out of the bottles. Fleece clothing is all made out of recycled bottles. In fact, we've been thinking, maybe moving furniture is good for your energy. Sure, and it might make you thirsty, and certainly bottled water tastes better, and it's good for you. It's pure. Look for pen and teller bullshit water available on the web for $150 a bottle. And it's not just for special occasions anymore. There you are. And all of those examples are well worth careful and closer study. Well, let's talk about money a little bit. You were promised a little bonus session on that, and I pulled some stuff for you. And we'll start here. A lot of the attraction of money from people, the getting of money from people, has to do with how you think and feel about money. And it maybe has as much to do with that as anything else. And move this away from me. And so there's sort of two overall overriding philosophies about money. One is that there is a finite amount of it, and that if you subtract some, the subtraction is permanent, and that if you get a dollar, it had to diminish someone else's store of dollars. In business, one of the reasons corporate clients are frustrating is two things. One is their philosophy about money includes the idea of budgets. So they like develop a advertising budget for the year in advance of the year. Well, what's wrong with that picture? It's a predetermined restriction of how much their sales can be for the entire year because they based it on a formulaic equation against their sales. So when really your advertising budget should be as much as you can possibly spend as fast as you can turn the money over, so sell more, advertise more, sell more, advertise more. Who knows how much you should spend on advertising this year because we don't know how much you're going to sell this year yet. So their budgeting is based on the idea of finite dollars. The other corporate thing that gets talked about is market share. And so everybody starts out with a predetermined idea that there's a size of a market. And so there's only so many people who are going to do college planning this year. And so there's only so many people who are going to get cosmetic dentistry this year. There's only so many people who are going to get coaching this year. There's only so many people and it all adds up to this. And now we're all fighting and worrying over our share of that market. But what happens if we can make the market bigger? Then all bets are off, right? In many respects, I was sorry to see him get canned. The last new CEO of Home Depot, the recently departed CEO of Home Depot, made the excellent point that Home Depots valuations, everybody's opinion about Home Depot was based on market share. But his argument was it's based on the do-it-yourself market. But the fastest growing and evolving market is the do-it-for-them market, not the do-it-yourself market. So what happens if we combine that market with the do-it-yourself market? And then there's the contractor market, which we haven't done a very good job with. So what happens if we put that in there? So now all the market share measurements are off. So when you think in these limited terms, you automatically set yourself up for limited results. And so a lot of people believe that there's a finite amount of money. So each customer or client has a finite amount of money that they are going to spend in a particular category in a year. And then what share of that are you going to get? Each customer or client has a finite amount of money that they're going to spend total. They have certain price points that they will not go past. And so your conversation constantly with people, and your conversation with your business, if you consult our coach, your conversation will incorporate statements made by the clients that, oh, our customers would never spend X, whatever that amount is. Our customers would never spend more than Y on this. Oh, customers in Beverly Hills might spend that much for this, but customers in Dufus West Virginia would never spend that much for this. All of these predetermined ideas about money. And they are all limiting statements of a client who is highly skilled, highly talented. All of the elements we would look for to be fabulously successful in the consulting coaching business. They should probably be doing 30 million a year. They're really doing about five. And if you strung them all together from a day, you would have about three hours of these limiting statements about what their customers won't do and why their customers won't pay X and all of this. Some years ago, I empirically proved to them that you could do pretty much what Ron Carothers did. That you could take their $1,000 price point and you could make it, say, $15,000 price point, and it mattered not. And so they've left it at $15,000. And if continued that part of their business, but all the other parts of their business, they're still back where they were before, of our customers will never spend more than this. Oh my God, our customers would never spend that much. And the 412 reasons why their customers wouldn't spend, including the fact that the customers don't have the money. And the best illustration of that is to, when you go home, go find the worst mobile home park in your community you can find. I mean, you want them, you know, the kids with no underwear on run around out in the front gravel in front of the parking thing and you want them up on blocks, lean into one side and a couple of good crack dealers in there. And I mean that kind of mobile home park where they are just falling apart. There will be satellite televisions. There will be dishes mounted on the sides to go to the 52 inch television set inside the mobile home, which actually the weight of the thing is knocking the mobile home off of its blocks. Because people have the money for what they really wish to spend the money on or what they decide to spend the money on. And almost without exception, we will underestimate their ability and willingness to spend. Now the nice thing about our businesses, most of you, well almost everybody in here probably they're coaching and consulting, has a direct ROI result to it. I mean our businesses increase their capacity to spend the more they buy. If they use it at all. Because they use it, they do better. Well, then they can buy more. They use it, they do better, they can buy more. And we should be thinking in terms of that cycle. The other interesting thing about this first several years I spoke at Joe Polish's boot camps for Carbaclaners. And there would all, every year, we did hot seats. And every year, after the first year, I started making fun of it ahead of time thinking that that would end it, but it never did. There would always be somebody who would come up for their hot seat and their contention was that all of their customers were cheapskates. And everybody in their community was poor. And everybody bought by price. And because competitors did it at two cents a square foot, they had to do it at two cents a square foot. By the way, as an aside, I hope you noticed the one getting the most money in Feng Shui had a pricing formula. Rather than quoting a fee, she had a mechanism for figuring out how much the fee should be, which in that case would have been $7,000 for every bail charge. Useful lesson. And so I would sit there and I'd say, well, if that be true about where you live, then there should only be one store, Walmart. That's it. There should be no other stores. Because if everybody in your community buys everything by price, you can't be at Walmart's price and they sell everything. So why would there be anything else? Is there a Sears? Is there a Neiman Marcus? Is there a Nordstroms? Is there a Macy's? Is there a Target? And when we drive down the street, the only car we should see is a Kia. Can't buy a cheaper car than a Kia. So it's the only thing we should see. So you guys got a Ford dealer over there? How can he possibly be staying in business? You got a GM dealer? You got him. And now if you've managed, if it's not the area, but it's your customers, and you've managed to round up all the cheapskates in your area, as your customer, what did you do to round up all the cheapskates? You picked them. So this whole thing about price and movement of money and how money moves mostly has to do with what you believe about price and money and how money moves. And it doesn't have to do with much else. My old friend Foster Herbert said more people have more hang-ups about money than even about sex. And he's probably right. So people come to our kind of business with an entire complicated belief system about money that mostly gets in their way. So I thought tonight we'd just talk about a few of those things. The funny thing is the actual way in which why money moves from one person to another in a society. And most people think it moves for reasons that it does not or that is controlled by things that do not control it. So here's a couple of great quotes. This is, let's show through them. Not real well, but money's like fire and element has little trouble by moralizing his earth, air, and water. Well, what does that mean? Well, it means houses of good people catch on fire, not just houses of bad people. Seemingly, the system would have been designed differently, don't you think? I mean, really, the only houses that ought to burn down are houses where evil, nasty child predators live. Why does the house of a really nice person with a good family burn down? Bad system, right? Well, because fire doesn't make any moral judgments. Fire sets fire to stuff that's ready to be set fire to. So the electric plug goes bad. Fire doesn't make a judgment of, oh, electric plug goes bad, but it's in the house of really nice good people. So we won't burn their house down. We'll wait till a plug goes bad in the house of some evil people. Fire doesn't do that. Money doesn't do it either. Money makes no moral judgments whatsoever. And so a lot of people have the idea that purely by being good people, they will prosper. That's not how money moves. Doesn't even move based on delivering a good service. That's not how money moves. I told you, the best adjustments I've ever got, the best chiropractors I know are the most impoverished. Because they don't understand what makes money move from one person to another in a society. They think it is the exceptional value of the core service that they deliver. And that's not what makes money move. Some of the things that do make money move, if you carefully observe, we ended talking about one of them today, celebrity makes money move. Money moves from people to celebrities. Now we can have a whole other conversation about their ability to keep it. That has to do with them. That doesn't have to do with the movement of money in the first place. But all you have to do now is pay attention to Forbes publishes a list each year, not the 400 richest people, but then they do a 400 richest list of celebrities. Go look at the numbers. Go look at how, see the actors, 30 years ago in Hollywood an actor was an employee. You hired him for a couple hundred thousand bucks a year. He made eight movies a year. He made the movies you told him to make. Not any more. That whole system is upside down. Pro-sports upside down. The money moves to the celebrity. Not necessarily to the most talented. We can all find more talented singers than Britney, who aren't getting the kind of money that is moving to Britney. We can find more talented athletes than the... How come a whole ton of money moved again to Terrell Owens? Why? Because stats actually suck. Drops as many as he catches. Why not? His drop service has had his catches. And the drops are critical times that catches aren't. Loses you more games than he wins. Ton of money moved to him. Why? Because he's DO. Not for any other reason. And he's still in Dallas and Bill isn't. Why? Because the money moves to the biggest celebrity. Money moves to the biggest promoters. Money moves is attracted by speed. Money is attracted by self-promotion. Money is attracted by something that is exciting. So money moves for, if you will, reasons of its own that don't have anything to do with moral judgment. The money doesn't go where it's most needed. If that happened, all of the real estate development of late would not have been in Las Vegas. It wouldn't have been in Miami. They were already very well developed. All the real estate development money would have gone to New Orleans and Bangladesh and Somalia. And everybody would be building high-rise condos in Somalia. If money went where it was most needed, it doesn't go where it's most needed. It goes for other reasons. So it is important to become an accurate thinker about how money moves in society and then to put those forces to work for you, not to either ignore them or to fight them. And certainly not to align yourself with false beliefs about how money moves. For a lot of people, the finite thought thing gets in the way because they believe that if they take $30,000 from this person, this person is now $30,000 poorer and has $30,000 less to spend. I use that number as an example because we know quite a few people who are in multiple $30,000 coaching programs in the same niche. Not only are they in one, they're in two, they're in three. In some cases, they're in four in the same niche. So how come the first guy taking $30,000 from them didn't paralyze them for the second guy and the third guy and the fourth guy? Variety of explanations for this, including the fact that a buyer is a buyer is a buyer is a buyer. All populations continually resort themselves to 5, 15 and 80. So there's 5% of every population who buys everything. There's 15% of every population who buys a lot. There's 80% of every population who buys nothing. And so in a given niche, if we take dentistry where Charlie is, in a given niche, if we go analyze who's in every practice management group and in every coaching group and in every client group, you know what we're going to find? 5% of the profession are in a crept load of them. 15% of them are in some of them and 80% of them are in none of them and are never going to be in any of them. Doesn't have anything to do with the money it has to do with human behavior. So the zero sum game doesn't really exist either. So let's talk about the forces that move money over which you have control. The first is what you decide. So loops to what we talked to you today. You make all the decisions that control whether money flows to you or whether it does not. Because these are the things that really control the flow of money. So you get to decide who you want to be. Nobody else needs to have any governance over that. We were talking at dinner, Lee and Carl and I and Sidney, exchanging where did you grow up and under what circumstances did you grow up. And the stories are interesting and if anything what they have in common is a sort of environmental need for resourcefulness at a young age. But fundamentally where you grew up has no impact whatsoever on what you decide today about who it is you are going to be. It doesn't matter whether you grew up in Manhattan or whether you grew up in Poughkeepsie or whether you grew up in West Virginia or whether you grew up in Bangladesh. Proof is by the way, so sometimes we will hear oh you know these poor people who are in the ghetto and their third generation welfare people and the only role models they ever see are drug dealers. And you know they don't have a chance except we can go find stories of people who have come out of that exact environment and are fabulously successful and fabulously wealthy. Now the losers amongst us think where there's one there's a freak accident. The winners think where there's one there's a formula, there's a model and there could be a hundred, there could be a thousand, there could be an unlimited number if they just followed the model. So where you grew up, the conditions you grew up under whether you had supportive parents or unsupportive parents, none of that impacts on what you decide about who you're going to be today. No predetermination. We've already established today experience doesn't matter, expertise doesn't matter. So none of that determines what you want to do today. Now we say look there are some real limits but they are far away from what most people operate under. I mean as a practical matter I can't go be the quarterback of the Dallas Cowboys. Cleveland Browns are the Oakland Raiders maybe. But you know the Dallas I'd probably get a tryout at least at the Raiders. But quarterback in the Cowboys is probably out you know. It's a little old to really start thinking about that. But I could buy them. I mean I could own them. But you know far within the real limits about what do you want to do. For most people what they want to do is well within their grasp. Sometimes those are the things that you want to be known for. Well you decide that. Nothing prior to today matters, doesn't have to control it. Nobody else gets to decide what that is. You can decide right now, tonight you can sit down with a pad and make a list of the five things you want to be known for. And go to work to become known for them. And make that happen. It's a very mechanical process. We've talked about a lot of it today. How you want to be perceived. How you want to conduct business and live. Who you want to associate with. Who you want to let into your world. Who do you not want to let into your world. One of my first speaking gigs and it's still one of my favorite stories about this. I was doing real basic personal growth motivational one on one stuff. And I was speaking for free any place they let me speak for you in Phoenix. And so a lot of the speaking was in office stuff. So I'm speaking in this real estate office that they're morning sales training meeting. And this is the time when meeting attendance was compulsory. So the brokerages could actually insist they'd be there. Can't do that today. And it was like eight people at eight desks. So they're all sitting at their desks. And I read in a newspaper and drinking coffee and all that stuff. I'm speaking as horrible. It's all over with this woman comes up to me and she says I I agree with everything you said but which is you know it's the but that gets you right. So I can't tell you I'd like to have a penny for every time I've heard I agree with everything you said but or you were right. But I'm still going to keep doing it the wrong way. In spite of the fact that you were right or not. So she comes up she's I agree with everything you said but I have this problem. What's your problem. I want to be a positive thinker. I'm telling I was doing very basic but the person at the desk next to me. Here's what they do every morning. They come to work to get the newspaper. They get coffee and they read aloud and talk about all the bad news that's in the newspaper. So that person every day gives me all the bad economic news. And fundamentally all of the reasons why I'm not going to be able to make any money today this week this month whatever. And this goes on for like a half an hour every morning. I said change desks. Move your desk. You know you're not ankle change to the guy. If he's a toxic person move if they won't let you change desks change offices. There's 10,000 real estate brokerages in Phoenix. They'll all welcome you and hang your license on the wall. I mean so you decide who you let in. You decide who you sit next to except on Southwest. You decide who you hang out with. You know we don't get to pick our relatives but you can even make some decisions there about at least how much time you spend. You decide where you want to work and live. I used to get that all the time when I lived in Phoenix. A traveling somebody on a plane. Oh you live in Phoenix. Oh I wish I could live in Phoenix. I live in Ruta Bega Minnesota where it's always 42 degrees below zero and there's 863 pounds of snow and oh this is awful. I said well they got them green signs overhead with arrows on them. They tell you how to get out of town. You don't even need a you know a wizard gizmo. There's signs. They show you leave. You know. I mean and you decide when you want to do things. So the first thing that makes money move to people is decision. Somebody in here mentioned LMI earlier to me on a break. I mean Paul Meyers great quote was you know if you're not getting what you want out of life it's probably because your goals aren't clearly enough to find. Well I mean he's basically right. It certainly is not for any shortage of opportunity. We just saw a bunch of complete fruit loops. Getting paid $3,000 to go in and move the furniture around. And it wouldn't surprise me if she was booked silent. It ain't for lack of opportunity. See one of the messages of what you just saw. The bottled water, the Feng Shui all of it. Is there's a ton of money out there moving around all the time. People their money is very fluid. Money's moving. There's all kinds of money out there. Go look. Walk around. Go to a mall. Go drive around a shopping center on Friday night. Try and get into a restaurant. Go to a when the hell the last time Carla were we in that Mac store. I mean I forget when we were there together but it was like an off day. It wasn't like Christmas or anything. I mean it was like dinner hour or something. You walk in this Mac store and I mean people are in line 20 deep at the registers with piles of this stuff. Laptops and gizmos. I mean you would think they were buying popcorn at Kmart. They're piling up this stuff. Big screen TVs. Can't keep them in stock. Best buy store in my neighborhood right before Super Bowl actually put a big sign up on the building. We're out of televisions. Because they just want any more people coming in because they were out of TV. It's over. We're out. Don't bother us. We've sold all the 52 inch HD TVs we can get our hands on before the Super Bowl. We got no more. We can't get any more. Don't even bother coming in. I don't live in Beverly Hills. I live in a very modest community in Ohio. There's money out there flowing like it's never been flowing. But it flows to the people who decide to take it. And to put in motion the factors that cause it to come to them. So the first thing is all about decision. So how much you want. How fast do you want to get it. Why not go from zero to a million instead of zero to 100,000. Why not double the business instead of increase the business incrementally by 10 percent. You just decide to do it. That's like my favorite Iacocas story. What is his story of the convertible where Chrysler is you know belly up as they are now again. So they're on the verge of permanently going south. There's no money. There's no money there under all kinds of pressure. He's in the midst of a turnaround. He's trying to raise money borrow money. He's got all kind of problems going on. There's no R&D going on. He's on the factory floor and three guys come over and say that card make a great convertible and there's no convertibles being sold. No American made convertibles on the road. In case you didn't know it Chrysler put him back on. Three guys say that card make a great convertible. Iacocas says get a blow torch cut the roof off. You know, you can drive around and see if girls are interested in it. If they are we'll make it. Now there's not you probably couldn't find another auto executive in the history of the auto industry who would do what he did. Especially under the conditions that he was under. Everybody else would organize committees, get the R&D guys in, do research, talk to the lawyers. What's going to be our litigation risk of convertibles. It would be like a three year development project. And by the time they got the thing on the road, if they got it on the road, the company be bankrupt or you know, there'd be 50 other convertibles on the road. No, he just said let's go find out if when we stop a traffic lights girls look at us. And if they do then we know guys will buy them. And they did so after they took turns driving around for a few days, then he called everybody office and said let's make them. No R&D, no let's make them. That's decision. And most people like never really make decisions about these issues. Who they really want to be. Who are they there, whoever they turned out to be. Not who they decided to be. What are they doing? They're doing some composite of what they turned out to be doing. If you ask most people why they're doing what they're doing. Well, it's what I went to school to do or it's what dad did or you know, I don't know how to do anything else. I hear that a lot, by the way, from ordinary people. This is all I know how to do. This is what you'll hear from all the auto workers who are losing their jobs. For 20 years everybody's known they're going to lose their jobs. They've had 20 years to learn a new job. This is all I know how to do. Well, how'd you get to be able to do this? I learned how to do it. Well, seemingly. If you learned how to do this, you could learn how to do something else, don't you think? I mean, so the first thing that starts money moving towards you is decision. It's making these kinds of decisions as clearly as you can. Another way to say it is clarity attracts money. Cloudiness, fuzziness does not attract money. So money goes where there is clear thought. There's an article in the... What's the local magazine called? Washington... Washington... whatever it is that you get. What is it? Washingtonian... Washingtonian, however you say it. It's a local city, you know, high-end magazine. There's an article in this issue about Steve Case, the guy that originally started AOL, who's got the new WISBANG health thing that he's doing. It's an article worth reading because he's very, very clear about what he intends to do. And so consequently, I invest, if there was a place to invest at the moment, because of his clarity. So that's the first thing that makes money move, is people making decisions about what it is that they're going to do and how they are going to do it and when they are going to do it and where they are going to do it. The second thing that causes money to move... I got my slides out of order. Here we go. I got my slides out of order, but I do. Is planting the decisions in the subconscious and then letting it do the work? The old... there's a... most of you by now have undoubtedly seen the much publicized and much promoted DVD thing, The Secret, which Oprah has adopted. I have many thoughts about it that we don't have time for tonight, but I will tell you that one of the most interesting things about it... how many in here have seen it? Okay, so everybody's seen it, everybody's heard about it. Hotest thing since sliced bread. One of the most interesting things about it to me is that it is an unacknowledged but unabashed and direct rip-off of Earl Nightingale's recording The Stranger Secret from 1960 something. To its core message being verbatim lifted from Earl Nightingale's The Stranger Secret, which is we become what we think about most. And it said in Earl Nightingale's recording and it is said in this thing as well. And it is both true and false. The old joke about it is if it's true, if we become what we think about most, I'm going to have a problem because in about another three hours I'm going to be a woman. So, you know, it's not exactly literal, right? What's unset about it should be, and neither the old message or the new message makes it very clear, is that it's not just about conscious thought, it's about subconscious thought. And so it's about what you plant subconsciously that the subconscious is now directed to work on. And if you plant clear decisions in the subconscious, then in ways that we could spend days instead of minutes debating and talking about, but in ways that it's not important to understand, the subconscious goes to work to pull all the resources and opportunities and connections and stuff. And so, together in order to make it happen, I've seen it over and over and over again in my own life pretty much to the point that you can plant it and then you can know if it's properly planted that all sorts of things are going to start happening in order to make it turn into reality. Now, again, since most people don't clearly decide these things, they can't actually plant them. But if you do, then attraction rather than pursuit, somebody asked me on a break, you did, without, I'll do a simplified, broader version of what you said to me. But he basically said, I'm getting all my business by chasing it. And I need to get a certain amount of business right now for continually for financial reasons. I can't really afford to take one step backward to take two steps forward, 90 days later. So I'm reluctant to stop the chasing. But how do I switch to attracting? Well, here's part of the answer is that the chasing is a very conscious process. The attraction is actually a very subconscious process. It's decide, plant, let it come to you. Now, again, salespeople, which he's a salesperson, I'm a salesperson, we're ingrained to chase. I mean, it's instinctive behavior. But we do it all on a conscious level. We make all those decisions on a conscious level. And a conscious is a much more powerful entity when you clearly plant what you want in it. So the person who has a bunch of cheap customers who won't pay X for what it is that they want, for example, they need to kind of back up and start here. They need to decide on what type of customer they want. They need to plant that decision, and pretty soon they'll start to attract those kinds of customers. And it really isn't much more complicated than that. The third thing that causes money to flow to you is really a pretty interesting one. It's ask. My friend, Mark Victor Hansen, one of the things that I've observed, and I think he's better at it than anybody. I've gotten better at it, but he is exceptional at this. He will ask anybody for anything. With no thought of reciprocity, no real reason why they should do it, no attempt to give them any reason to do it necessarily, he'll just ask them. And amazingly, a good percentage of the time, they give them what they ask for. And so most people don't get, because they don't ask. They don't like put it out there, and they don't place demands on everybody. And in the business that we talked about earlier today, the coaching and consulting business, you know, you have to get very good at placing demands on people. Because you really are going to need a lot of people to work for you. You need a lot of people to refer to you. You need people to multiply on your behalf. You need people to spread the word about you. You need people to comply. You've got to be really, really good at placing demands on people. The little clip out of the muse. I mean, she moves right from, I'm accepting you as my client, to sweet at the four seasons on a high floor. Here's my dietary list, and oh, by the way, take the dry cleaning over there on your way there. Keys are client. And don't chalk it up to, oh, it's a fictional, funny movie. Don't look at it that way. Look at it like an unintentional sales training film for consultants, because that's what it is. And so the placing of demands on people is a way to get greater control over them. Money moves to people who ask and place demands. That's who money moves to. Money does not move to people who wait for it, or are timid, or are patient, or all of that. That's not how money moves. Money is attracted to people who ask and place demands. That means, by the way, you need to develop a system for this. You need to develop a process that you use repeatedly over and over and over and over again for asking people, for obligating people, for giving recognition for what they do, for rewarding them not necessarily, monetarily. And then they will continue to give you money. One of my earliest clients said to me, your fee is too low. And he said, don't raise mine, but raise everybody else's. Right? 1974. Best thing I ever heard, because he was right. Right? And so your fee is too low. And people won't take you seriously because it is too low. Raise your fee, you'll be taken more seriously. So it's not just about get more money by raise fee. It's raise fee in order to convey the right message in order to get more money. There's more to it than just raising prices. Money moves because of all of these forces that you set in motion. And you set them in motion. The last big thing that makes money move is to act. So some people manage to get decisions made, but then they don't like, ever like, they don't ever really, you know, take the blowtorch, cut the roof off, and two days later decide to make the damn car. So if you're in a business with supreme and wholly unreasonable confidence, start things with no idea of how the hell they're going to be completed or being ready to do that. If you wait for the, if you wait to get organized, so you're not in the money attraction business, you're in the getting organized business. A person in coaching finally got rid of him this year, but I had him for three years. And every conversation was all about what he was going to do. Key words, going to. Not what I'm doing, going to. Another guy, he's my favorite. Two years, phone call every month to talk about the sales letter. No, no, no, the same sales. No, the sales letter that never got mailed. He was working on the sales. Trying to develop the perfect sales. And after two years, he still sending me, you know, 18 new drafts to look at this month before the phone call about the sales. Well, first of all, at this point, I don't care. Second, secondly, at this point, you couldn't, I can't even tell anymore what's good and what's bad. It's impossible. Okay. I mean, and third of all, we could have found out more two years ago by mail on some of them. I mean, buy some stamps, you know, get the thing in the mail. Well, I'm waiting for this, I'm waiting for that. In the ready-made million-year system, I talk about the fact that the wealthy don't do anything sequentially. They do a whole bunch of things simultaneously. And so it's opposite of how we're all taught to behave, right? And so if you think about money in general, here's what everybody tries to do. They try and get wealthy sequentially, meaning they try and make some money. And then when they're making more money than they need to eat, house, etc., then they try and save some money. Then when they've saved up enough money to make it worse, skirt around with investing, then they finally get around to investing money. But the sequential thing, they never get there. You got to make save, invest all at the same time, because you got to take it off the top of every dollar that comes in. People do business the same way. They try and do everything sequentially. But that's not how you get big things done, and that's not how you attract money to you. Because money is attracted to something going on that is big and exciting and dramatic and dynamic and chaotic. It's not attracted to the tiny, timid, methodical, sequential things. You like, don't notice any more than the rest of the world notices. If you look at Trump, I think one of the most interesting things about Trump, of all of the things that are interesting about Trump, is that nobody makes more affirming statements per minute than he does. Watch him now when he's interviewed. I mean, people make fun of it. They don't get what he's doing, so they make fun of it. But every statement's an affirming. Every project's going to be the greatest. It is going to be the greatest project anybody's ever seen. It's going to be the greatest golf course ever built on a planet Earth. Oh, this is going to be the greatest season. This is going to be the greatest show. This is fabulous. This is terrific. Listen to him. And everything's big. Tallest building, biggest building, biggest golf course, biggest golf course built on a slant, biggest this, grandest this, greatest that. Well, you get people really excited by saying, you know, we're building the 19th best thing, you know, or the 27th biggest thing. You don't draw much attention with that and you don't attract money with that. So you act, but you got to act in a big way to inspire yourself and others. Larger than life. Remember, that's what people want. So that's the process for putting all the forces in motion that move money to you. I would like to talk a little bit more about price with you. Price is sort of a representation of how people think about money. So what did you think this morning? I asked rhetorically, you can answer to yourself, but what did you think this morning? What ran through your mind when Corruthers said, $1100, we raised the price to $30,000? Well, what would run through most people's minds is of course all sorts of negative things. I don't believe it. There must be more to it than that. I could never get away with that with my product service customers market. He must be doing something unethical, illegal, criminal, deceptive, fraudulent, etc. But all it really says is the $1100 was the wrong price. That's all it says. It doesn't say anything more than that, doesn't say anything less than that. It says the $1100 was a gross miscalculation of what the market would bear and what the market perceived the service as being worth. It doesn't say anything more than that, not more or less. All it says is the $1100 price was wrong. And his example is not unusual. I have seen it and or engineered it again and again and again and again and with those kind of multiples where somebody had so grossly undervalued what it is they were selling or underestimated their market. Years ago I had a guy in coaching, Larry McIntyre. I don't know what's come of Larry, but Larry was in coaching in Larry's business. Most of you have heard me mention it. Larry's business was finding foreign brides, helping American men find foreign brides. And he came to the first coaching meeting and described the business to give you a quick frame of reference. The business has been around forever. And if you open up USA Today on any one given day and look at the classifieds, you get to say three or four ads, one of them is headline, foreign brides. You'll see three or four ads for these services. And most of them are really sort of in the list business. They're pretty cheap, 30 bucks, 50 bucks, 500 bucks, give or take. And basically they give the guy a list. And now he emails and starts corresponding and so forth. So Larry's business was a little more elaborate than that. He had ground troops on the ground in 35 countries screening the women. The women of course entered the database for free. In his case they had to speak some English. They had to be professed Christians. They had to pass a health test. And they had to be good looking. And so there's thousands and thousands of women in this database because they all want to get to the United States. And then the guys pay. So Larry comes and describes the whole thing to us. And I didn't have to do it. Other people in the coaching group started it because his price at the time was $395. And for this he screened the guy. He got the guy into the database, starts the guy communicating. And then when the guy finds some he wants to go meet, Larry organizes trips and takes him over there and on and on and on. 395 bucks. And everybody in the coaching group went, are you out of your mind? I didn't even have to do it. LeGrand probably had the most effect on anybody. Ron you could have scraped off the ceiling when he was here. He's just screaming. I mean it's not like you're arranging a date here. This guy's looking for a life. I mean this is like a big life decision here. You know it's 395 bucks. Surely they'll pay more than that. Larry says, well I compete with these other guys. I mean so now comes the list. You know a lot of my customers are blue collar people and I compete with these guys who are on the highest price of anybody already. You'll hear that a lot. I'm already the highest priced guy. What does that have to do with anything? Nothing. That's not a test of the market. So we fussed and fussed and fussed with Larry and we finally convinced him to raise his price. So how much do you think I should raise the price by finally? And I'll test it. I said well we don't have any real basis for making a decision here so let's do something easy. Let's just add a zero. And then we don't have to do any complicated math. So we'll just make a 3950. Oh customers, no difference in conversions. Same story you heard this morning. No difference in conversions, same number of leads turned into buyers. Nothing new added, nothing changed. Just added a zero. Is that everything else to say? And we started to do some real work and you know find out where the customers were coming from and who the customers were and narrow the demographics and match the offer to the demographics and then add telemarketers and then add differential pricing so it's $39.95 if you get Larry's team working with you but if you want Larry working with you it's $7,995. Of course 20% of people take the $7,995 thing. 80% take the $3,995 thing. I've seen it again and again and again and again. And so what's the barrier to price? Well the first barrier to price is you understand you set your own price. Competition doesn't set price, comparable. Now people let it, but competition doesn't set price, comparables don't set price, past experience doesn't set price. What your father sold stuff for when he ran the business doesn't set price. The most amount of money your father ever made in a year doesn't have anything to do with price. Nothing has to do with price except what you decide about what price ought to be and whether or not the marketplace will respond to it when it is put in front of it. When I came into the information products business, Daniel Conan was the gold standard of the business. Daniel Conan has a pricing formula, $10 a tape, $10 a CD now, forgive me. That's their belief about price. And everybody else in the industry followed them because they are the gold standard. So pretty much everybody selling stuff for $10 a CD as the standard. My very first product, it was awful. And I didn't know squat, but it was $30 a tape. Why? No rational, well I'll tell you why, because I needed the $60. I mean, somebody asked me on the dinner break about the influential writing workshop. How did you come up with that price? Well actually, yes, how do you justify the price? Which of course I don't, but the answer to the question, well it's really up to you. You're buying it, but I mean how do you come up with the price? Well it's very simple. There's going to be 24 people in a group. I won't work for less than X amount of dollars for that amount of time. We divide the dollars I got to have by the 24 people. That's the price. It's not having to do with, I could give a rat's patootie what this seminar sold for, that seminar sold for, this guy's doing anything for, none of that is doing anything. You set your own price. And only you. Then you take it to market. And guess what you will find. In most cases you will find that there is a market for almost every price. There are people paying $18,000 for a pair of shoes. I remember shortly before my father died, I told him, he asked how much I paid for the car. I just bought it and he said, for that price, well he said something crude. He said for that price, I ought to have a back porch and it ought to, and I won't tell you the rest of what he said. But see that's his frame of what is. And we all have things that to us, what other people are paying for them is just ludicrous to us. So the shoe thing to me. But I mean I've paid $1,000 for those shoes. But $18,000 for a pair of shoes. Handbags. Women's handbags. I'll bet you guys don't even know. I'll bet you have no idea. It's a damn purse. I mean they're getting $4,000, $5,000, $6,000 for these things. And they don't even have them locked in the glass cases anymore. That's the ordinary normal price stuff. That ain't even the good stuff under the counter. And there's a market for it. So price to market, there's a market for just about everything. So you decide your price. You take it to market. And you resist all attempts by others to regulate your price. And of course the sickest thing, the sickest feeling is recognizing the fact that you've been underpricing and underselling all this time. So two things happen simultaneously for Ron. When he discovers they'll pay $30,000, he's very excited for about five minutes. Then he spends the next 15 minutes multiplying the $29,000 he didn't get by the 100 clients he took care of the year before. Which by the way is a lot of money. Right? Oh yeah, you're welcome. And how many years you've been doing this? But you know, he just didn't know. Who knows till then. So you've got to understand, price is primarily your responsibility and your responsibility only. And there's a market for just about everything. Try not to say about price. In a few minutes we have a lot that I think is always useful to remind people is that transaction size matters a lot. And it matters for a lot of reasons. Not just its gross differential. So discovering that your transaction size can be $30,000 instead of $1100 is in and of itself a very big discovery. But from the standpoint now of growing the business, dominating the marketplace, you just got $29,000 more that you can spend divided between the experience delivered to the customer and the marketing of the thing to get the customer and you could spend it all and be right back in the same position you were before. So if you cut it in half and you put $14,000 in your pocket that you didn't have before, you now have $14,000 more. So you can spend $7,000 of it upping the experience for the client and making it an extraordinary experience. And you can spend the other $7,000 marketing the thing and you didn't have that budget before. $7,000 times 100 transactions in a calendar year is $700,000, right? Am I wrong? Right. So we just created a $700,000 marketing budget and a $700,000 client experience budget purely by changing the transaction size. Well, now what happens? Well, the experience is so much better. Your reputation will shall precede you. The word of mouth will be so much better that we can turn around and raise the price again. The $7,000 marketing budget allows us to do just about anything. The $1100 transaction size really restricts us. Speed. You get to each million you want a lot faster in $10,000 clumps than in $1,000 clumps. You get there even faster in $30,000 clumps than in like three times faster. So if you want to talk about speed, see speed is often purely a function of transaction size. It's a lot easier to make a million dollars as a copywriter charging $100,000 per gig than it is charging $15,000 gig. Same gig. Same amount of work. You just get to the million faster. You're like done by March. So transaction size is really a very important thing. And even little bumps in transaction size can be meaningful. Earlier we used the example of him with $2,700 and said they'll pay $30,000. Well, they certainly pay $32,940. Won't make any difference. But $2,940 times $100, well, doesn't make any difference to the customer. It makes a lot of difference to the seller. How do anybody think that? They stop at the 99 cents. Well, if they'll pay $2,99, they'll pay $349. It probably won't make any difference. But while we're on Disney and look at the pricing, they got like $14 for a cap. Why? Well, they created an environment where they got captive custom. I mean, they did certain things to set that in motion to be able to sell a cap for $14 and not have anybody grumpy about it. Then any real mystery to what they do, you can see what they do. There's nothing that's invisible. I did this weekly facts last year, the title of the weekly facts for the good gold facts is all secrets are visible. There are. It's just most people don't pay an engine to them, right? So money's not that mysterious. It's like visible what people do in order to attract money to them. It's just most people then don't copy the method and mostly because they have all this emotional nonsense going on, you know, about it. And so last, let me talk about an interesting piece of emotional nonsense and then we'll conclude for the evening. A lot of people pull their economic punches because they're not liked. Wherever you went our conversation on the break of a lot of breaks, we had a conversation with one of the IVAs who's encountered somebody who. And she went upset about it, by the way, so don't misunderstand, but somebody who she told him she used the Kennedy name and the great reaction she got to this powerful brand that we've developed is her prospect. Oh, I know the Kennedy thing and I think they're slimy. And I said, well, I think it'll be the last time you're going to hear it. I mean, because the aggressiveness causes that reaction from some people. And so the old Willie Lohman syndrome is want to be liked, right? This is Donnie Deuchess quote, you know, it's a constant throughout my life. I've always had people who don't know me say from a distance. I really don't like that guy. In the NSA market where I did a lot of work, I used to get it all the time. And I would gradually hear the same thing he heard at the end, you know, somebody wind up doing business with you. You know, for years, I never met you. I didn't really know what you were buying like you. Well, do you worry about that or do you not worry about that? Well, money doesn't move to you because you are liked. Money does not move to you because you are disliked by certain people. Money moves to you for other forces. There's lots of reasons why you won't be liked if you focus on making a lot of money in business. So here's one new kid on the block after we've been laboring at this for years. By the way, there's a version of this in politics right now. If anybody thinks that Jesse Jackson and Al Sharpton are jumping up and down with joy over Obama, not happening. A bunch of folks aren't happy that they've been laboring for the last 30 years and this kid has come out of nowhere and actually might even have a shot at the crown. They ain't his biggest fans. Not happy. Who does he think he is anyway? And how dare he get to where they want to be faster but not pay your dues? Well, those are all reasons why you won't be liked. The more visible you are, the more successful you are, the faster you move, the more speed you create, the more money you attract to you, the more you will get this from some people. Here's what's really important to know about it. You're getting it from people who wouldn't give you money anyway. So what? They don't matter. Big thing culturally is charging for what others give away. How dare you do that? There's a cultural attitude in some associations, for example, that you may belong to where you're because you're all members of the same thing, but you're supposed to share and everybody's supposed to give everybody something for it. All their wisdom and expertise is everything for free because we're all brothers in the same, you know. And so there's resentment when you figure out how to make money from it. If you spit on cherished traditions or icons, which you probably will have to in order to make money. You say provocative, critical or offensive things, which is a great way to attract attention. In fact, money to you. If you make demands on money, just making a lot of money. Lots of reasons for a lot of people not to like you. And a lot of people worry about all that, but the truth of the matter is everybody who doesn't like you for all of those reasons are people who would not be giving you money anyway. And so what? Your opinions do not count. The greatest attractant of money that I know of is immunity to criticism. There's a great Abraham Maslow quote. I'm sure you all know Maslow. Here it is. High achievers leave independent of the good opinion of others. Now remember, we're in the business of dispensing gold stars. That's a business we're in. Good news is it's cheap currency because you get to print it legally. We're in the business of dispensing gold stars to others who need them from us in order to feel good about themselves. It's very important not to be one of them, but to be the superior being who does not need gold stars from other people. In order to feel good about ourselves. We're in the business of showing people ladders to climb. It's just very important that we refuse to climb any ladders. So we're in the business of encouraging behavior on the part of other people that we, as the superior beings, have to not engage in. And so the greatest attractant of money at all is being completely independent of the good opinion of others. It, like, liberates all the money to flow to you. Because all of the stuff you do that blocks money is all tied to trying to get the good opinions of other people. Why don't you charge more? Well, because people will criticize me, think less of me, they may not like me, somebody who needs my service may not be able to afford me. It's all about having the good opinion of others. Everything we do to block money is about what other people will think of us and what other people will think of us. Largely, those other people are the people who wouldn't give us money under any circumstances whatsoever. So it's about trying to get gold stars from other people. As soon as you decide you don't want or need any gold stars from anybody else and you do not care what they think of you and what it is that you do. And the only people you care about are the people who are voting with their wallets. You are liberated to do everything that attracts money to you. Until you are where Maslow described. You will make decisions about getting gold stars instead of getting money. You will make decisions about trying to please mom, mother-in-law, dead parent, neighbor, peer, peer group, peer association, employee, etc., etc., etc., rather than about getting money. As soon as none of your decisions have anything to do with getting gold stars, the good opinions of other people, then all of your decisions get made for profit reasons and everything else is out of the way. It's like the ultimate liberation and it's all in here. Trump, Sidney was telling me, it's not the first time I've heard it from a New Yorker. Trump is like magnetic everywhere but in New York. New Yorkers by and large dislike him intensely and universally say bad things about him. He's not really a draw in New York. I don't know it for fact but I would wager that the apprentice ratings are at their lowest in the New York market. There's the hard to be a profit in your home land thing but it's more than that. For all of the reasons not to like people list, the New Yorkers, many have decided not to like him for all of those reasons. If he had any concern about it whatsoever, it would color all of his decision making and be in his way. The representation of that is of course, well, the hair. I mean, he really does have enough money to fix it. The handling of the media. If there is ever a guy who is immune to criticism, he pays no attention. And so in his hometown, he is reviled. Everywhere else, he is celebrated and he is magnetic to money. He is not reviled by the people who invest money in the projects of his size or the people who wish to attach his name to their projects because it is in fact magnetic to money. And it is abundantly clear, purely from observation, that he could not care less about others' opinions of him. At least unless they are giving him money. When he spoke at Callers event, he did Q&A. And that's risky with him by the way. His reputation precedes him. He has a reputation of, shall we say, being temperamental and honest. And so he did Q&A, 15 minutes of Q&A at the end. And I thought he was very gracious and genuinely attempting to be helpful to people except one poor woman. She asked a remarkably dumb question. There's lots of ways to handle that when it happens. His was to say, you have asked a remarkably dumb question. And quite frankly, it's a giant waste of your opportunity to ask me a question. And it would be a giant waste of everybody else's time for me to answer it. Next question. Now, he's being paid a lot of money to be there. He could be concerned, you know, client, audience, word of mouth, and no concern about any of that. Not concerned that he irritates her, not concerned, you know. I spoke some years ago at a seminar where we did hot seats and I reduced a woman to tears. It wasn't my objective, by the way. I actually thought I was being rather gentle, but everybody's perceptions are different, you know. So she is out in the hallway crumpled up against a wall weeping with people consoling her. I don't do boot camps that much anymore, but should you have me speak, you might want to think twice about having me do a hot seat for people. But I was asked, you know, aren't you? I said no. I mean, I feel bad for her, you know, it wasn't my intent to do it. But I don't care what she thinks about me and I don't care about the people who think I'm evil and nasty because of what that. I don't care about any of that. What I care about is the people in the room who are giving me money, who are coming and buying things and signing up for consulting appointments. I don't care about that. She was going to reduce herself to tears over that. She wasn't going to give me any money anyway. And the four people out there spending their time consoling her and missing what's going on in the room, they weren't going to give me any money anyway. And the five that I magnetically attracted like there was no tomorrow, the reason I magnetically attracted them was for the same things I said that reduced her to tears. Had I been so gentle, I wouldn't have reduced her to tears. I wouldn't have attracted the five. Either. So I don't care. See, I don't care about, as a speaker, I never, I outlawed evaluation forms. I don't want them. I don't want to see them. Never did want to see them. Refused to let anybody I speak for use them for me. Why? Because I don't care. Don't you want to improve? No. I don't want to, I don't want to improve. No. No. Now, maybe I want to improve, but if I want to improve, here's what I'm going to do as a speaker. I'm going to go find somebody who's really, really good at selling from the platform, like Ronald Grand or Tommy Hopkins, and I'm going to ask them, and if necessary, pay them to evaluate me. Why? Because they're qualified to evaluate me. So their opinion I would value, even if it was critical. The 300 people in the room, they're qualified to do. They're qualified either to buy something or not buy something. That's what they're qualified to do at this point. And the only real evaluation is, who gave you money? Nice remarks, bad remarks on an evaluation form. See, that doesn't mean anything. It's free to fill out an evaluation form. What matters is you give me money. So the biggest thing you can do for yourself to be able to use all the mechanical things we're talking about is work on this one thing and one thing about your head. Understand that you are in the business of dispensing gold stars to people who need them in order to feel good about themselves. And by the way, it doesn't matter whether the CEOs of Fortune 500 companies, whether they make a million dollars a year, or whether they make 25,000, doesn't make any difference. They need gold stars from you in order to feel good about themselves. So you are in the business of dispensing them. But you have to be completely devoid of need for them. Because otherwise you spend all your time chasing the gold stars. And you make all your decisions about the gold stars. And you worry about the decisions you're making and how they will affect whether or not you're going to get a gold star. That's no way to make choices about business. What you make choices about business is by money. That's the biggest single thing you can do for yourself. What I'm going to be doing this morning is I'm going to be doing a couple things. So number one is I'm going to be talking to you about the ten big lessons that I've learned from 33 years in the coaching and consulting business, which in and of itself is kind of like scary that I've been doing this thing for 33 years. And I'll tell you about that in just a second. And then we're going to spend about 15 minutes or so at the end of the hour that I'm spending with you today. If anybody wants to come up to the table, one of the things that Dan asked me to discuss with you is any kind of structure questions. So how do you structure things? Specifically group coaching sessions. So if you have any questions about structuring that, I know a lot of you, a lot of the people in the room here are in either my Info Mastermind group or my VIP group. So you kind of see how I do a lot of the structuring already. And those of you who have been in Dan's Platinum group or VIP groups in the past or VIP millionaire or whatever else he's had, you've seen how he's done it. The structuring of mine and the structuring of his are very similar in fact. So just to let you know a little bit about my 33 years in 1974 is when I first went into the consulting business and actually as no surprise to all of you in the room, my initial consulting was in retail. And my expertise in 1974 believe it or not was I worked with retailers who were liquidating their business. So I was the hired, like hired gun that was coming in and worked with in their businesses to help them to liquidate their businesses. And I did this through all the 70s and also through the 80s. And I grew the largest retail liquidation company in the Mid-Atlantic area. And it was really was the lion's share of my revenue back then, more so than my own retail business was. And I was the guy that came in and put up all the orange signs that you see on the windows and all that other stuff. And I actually also turned it into a course where I had a seven-step system for liquidating a retail business and I would have people come in and they would hire me on different levels. One level was we'd come in and do the wholesale for them. And another level they would do, they could hire me on the lowest level, which is for two hours, they could come in and I would teach them how to do it themselves over two hours. And so I did that through the 70s and the 80s. In 1999, after having lunch with Dan in 1998, and having this lunch where I thanked him for helping me grow my retail business, and he was the one that said to me at that time, you know, Bill, you're doing such great stuff in your retail business, you should be teaching this to other menswear retailers. In 1999, I started my BGS marketing system. And with the BGS marketing system, I provided then and still provide today a very painful one-on-one coaching for an hour. If you invest in the BGS marketing system, you get a certificate with me for one-on-one for an hour, which is very painful. And thank God very few people take advantage of those certificates. But I do that. I also sell coaching through BGS marketing. And the way that I sell coaching through BGS marketing is I do it on what I call a two-hour minimal, which is we have a questionnaire that we send. And I do that on the questionnaire that we send. When somebody requests coaching, coaching call with me, they, we send them out a questionnaire, and they fill out the questionnaire, which they tell us, which is the questionnaire that I've developed, which they tell me all about their business, and they tell me about their specific marketing strategies. And they send it to me, and I review that for up to an hour. So that's the first of the two hours that I get on the phone with them, and I discuss it with them for the other hour. So that's the two hours. I charge $1,000 an hour. It's $2,000 minimal in order for me to do that for people. What that does is a couple of things. Number one is when I get on the phone with them, they don't spend a whole lot of time with me getting me up to speed. And usually that getting me up to speed is very painful because they don't know how to tell me things. So this, the other thing it does, which is the second thing is it prepares them to how to deliver information to me. So that's how I do that. It's a two hour process, one hour. I review it one hour on the phone with them, and that's $2,000. I also, in the BGS marketing system, if you open up your, your, your manuals on page 193. On page 193, you will see the sales letter that I wrote to sell the, a six month telecoaching program that has now, this coming Monday night is my fifth month of that. And so I have two more sessions to deliver, and you'll see the actual sales letter that I wrote in order to provide that. You'll notice something of, and Dan might be showing you some of these examples yet again today, but you'll notice something interesting on page 196 of the sales letter. And on page 196 of the sales letter, you'll notice that I give a 100% satisfaction guarantee on the telecoaching program. This is the only program that I ever have offered in coaching where I give a, where I have ever given a money back guarantee. And the only reason why I provided it here was because of the fact that I do not work very hard with my BGS marketing system today. I spend most of my time at Glaser Kennedy Inner Circle. So my relationship with my BGS marketing customers, members actually, is not as closely knit as it is with, with my Glaser Kennedy Inner Circle members. So therefore, they don't know me as well because I don't communicate with them as much. And so therefore I felt as though that was important to take, to some, to take away any kind of reluctance they might have to join this program. And actually, the funny story about this program is, is that this program was actually forced upon me sort of because Keith Lee who's back somewhere over in that area over there, I spoke at his event in Seattle earlier, late, or at the end of September, wasn't it? Of O4? And I offered this program to all the people that invested in my marketing system then, and I figured, well, and I think we picked up about 40 people roughly into it into that then, and I figured well if I'm going to do it, I might as well look as many on the call as possible so I went back to the BGS marketing system members and I added another hundred and some people from my own membership into this. So if I was going to deliver it for 40 people, I might as well deliver it for 150 people. So that's the reason why even this program exists. But the lesson I wanted to share with you about this is the fact that depending on relationship depends on whether or not you need to add the guarantee. I have a guarantee, no other programs that I'm involved with and I'll show you the rest of them in just a moment, do we have any kind of a money back guarantee at all. Also with BGS marketing, I also provide one day consulting, full one day consulting which is $10,000 for a full one day consult. And I also, with BGS marketing, I have in that company, I have what I call my retainer clients. My retainer clients are $5,000 a month and I meet with them for a half a day a month. We actually have, and these are with corporations, large companies. And I meet with them, they come to me, I spend a half a day with them each month and we basically go through what's going on in their business. We develop a task list between what needs to be accomplished between now and the next time we meet. They take that task list with them. That task list is not for me, that's for them. And I have several of those clients, I have three of those right now that are paying me $5,000 a month and they're a half a day a month and they are what they are.
